role_hints.oews_basis: 43-4051 Customer Service Representatives 25.9% of wage bill + 41-9041 Telemarketers 4.4% + 43-3011 Bill and Account Collectors 4.9%; note the record's role_mix is 4-digit 5614, which dilutes agent share with other business-support codes — at 561422 proper, agent occupations plausibly run 55-70% of the wage bill
role_hints.approx_share_of_wage_bill: ~35% as listed; likely 55-70% at the 6-digit code — verify against any 6-digit staffing evidence
role_hints.note: This is the core exposure: AI voice and chat agents now handle routine inbound/outbound interactions end-to-end (order status, collections reminders, tier-1 support, outbound qualification). The residual is escalation, empathy-critical, and complex multi-system cases — the run should size that residual from deflection-rate evidence, not assume it.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-1011 First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers 5.5%
role_hints.note: QA scoring and coaching analytics are already AI-saturated (100% interaction scoring vs sampled); span-of-control widens. Live escalation handling persists.
Workforce management, forecasting and reporting automate substantially; client relationship management persists.

role_hints.role: Sales (client acquisition for the bureau itself)
role_hints.note: Enterprise BPO selling is relationship/RFP work; proposal production automates.

role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1232 Computer User Support Specialists 1.6% + tech tail
role_hints.note: Desktop/telephony support automates; CCaaS platform integration work grows with AI deployment.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-2021 Counter and Rental Clerks 1.7% + 31-9094 Medical Transcriptionists 1.25% + 43-9051 Mail Clerks 1.5% — these look like artifacts of the 4-digit 5614 proxy (document prep, other business support), not real 561422 staffing
role_hints.note: Treat with caution: the proxy mixes in sibling-code occupations. Routine clerical work is near-fully automatable wherever it genuinely exists in contact centers.

source_hints.source: ContactBabel US Contact Center Decision-Makers' Guide (annual)
source_hints.why: The standard operational benchmark for US contact centers; recent editions carry AI/automation deployment and deflection-rate data usable for current_adoption_pct.

source_hints.source: CCW Digital (Customer Contact Week) market study series
source_hints.why: Practitioner surveys on AI agent deployment and human-agent headcount plans.

source_hints.source: Everest Group CXM services research (PEAK Matrix and market reports)
source_hints.why: Tracks outsourced CX pricing models (per-FTE, per-transaction, outcome-based) and the shift under gen AI — directly evidences who keeps the savings.

source_hints.source: Public CX/BPO comparables: Teleperformance, Concentrix, TTEC filings and disclosed deal multiples
source_hints.why: Exit-tier multiples and, in their own disclosures, evidence of AI cannibalization of per-hour revenue; TTEC/Concentrix trading multiples show how the market prices the melt.

source_hints.source: Site Selection Group contact center labor and site reports
source_hints.why: Domestic facility and operator landscape context; small-operator demographics are thin — expect to estimate owner age from generic small-business data.
Independent tracking of CX outsourcing volumes and AI-driven contract restructuring.

capture_questions: Outsourced contact-center work is billed per agent-hour, per FTE, per minute, or per transaction — when AI deflects contacts or cuts handle time, the billed unit itself disappears. Is there evidence operators are successfully re-pricing to outcome/resolution-based contracts and keeping the spread, or do competitive rebids pass essentially all AI savings (and the revenue base) to clients?

Enterprise clients run multi-vendor RFPs on 2-3 year contracts and own the customer data and telephony stack — what stops a client from licensing AI voice agents directly from CCaaS/AI vendors (Genesys, NICE, Five9, or model providers) and cutting the bureau out entirely?

Does an incumbent bureau own anything defensible for the AI era — domain-trained workflows, integrations, compliance certifications (HIPAA, PCI), licensed collections operations — or is its asset simply trained labor that AI replaces?

The size distribution is barbell-shaped (315 firms at 500+ employees vs 926 micro firms): do small bureaus serving SMB clients have stickier relationships and less sophisticated clients (slower bypass), or are they simply subscale and first to lose volume?
terminal_value_question: A telemarketing/contact-center bureau's product is human conversation sold by the hour or seat — no license, statute, or physical act stands between the client and an AI voice agent that does the same job. At mature AI capability, does paid demand for outsourced *human* agents survive at all, and at what volume — i.e., is AI here a tool that cuts the operator's delivery cost, or is the AI agent the direct replacement of the product the operator sells? Force the analysis to size the residual (escalation, empathy-critical, regulated-disclosure calls) and to say what happens to per-unit pricing on that residual; note also that domestic bureaus already lost the cost-arbitrage war to offshore once.
special_notes: Pricing-model deflation is the central mechanism, not a side effect: per-hour/per-FTE/per-minute billing converts every AI efficiency gain into fewer billed units. Model this explicitly in pi_dist; an operator 'adopting AI' here is often shrinking its own revenue.

Melting-base check: verify QCEW employment and SUSB firm-count trends for 561422 before assuming a stable acquisition universe — n_total is only 2,299 firms and the band (201, all in the 100-499 class) sits exactly where AI-driven volume loss hits hardest.

Boundary confusion: 561421 Telephone Answering Services is a sibling with different (small-account, subscription) economics; in-house corporate contact centers are outside this code entirely — most 'contact center AI adoption' surveys cover in-house centers, so check whether a statistic describes buyers of BPO or the BPO operators themselves.
The record's role_mix is a 4-digit 5614 proxy — occupations like medical transcriptionists and mail clerks are sibling-code artifacts; re-weight toward agent occupations for V.

Offshore delivery (Philippines, India, LatAm nearshore) competes with domestic seats and is invisible in US NAICS data; domestic revenue can melt via offshoring as well as AI — do not attribute all decline to AI.

EBITDA quirks: facility leases for seat capacity, high attrition/training costs, and client-concentration risk (one lost logo can be 20%+ of revenue for a mid-size bureau) — quality-of-earnings matters more than the multiple.
